text: Hello friend, today we are going to present a case of laparoscopic CVD exploration with cholecystectomy. This is a case of a seventy two year old female patient presented with severe pain in upper abdomen and she was investigated at some other centre and which was diagnosed her as obstructive jaundice due to cholecystitis and cholelithiasis.

and they subjected this patient for ERCP by medical gastroenterologist who tried to extract the stone from CVD but unable to do so. So he put a ten French plastic stain and the procedure is abandoned and referred this patient to us for further management.

Previous MRCP was suggested of cholelithiasis with dilated tortuous cystic duct. and which was inserting on the posterior wall of the superior pancreatic CBD containing few gallstones as well and IHBR was dilated and CBD and CHD was having multiple small and large calculi and

Here is a report of ERCP wherein the medical gastroenterologist has mentioned that he has tried to extract the stones with swiping balloons but still he cannot able to do that and hence he just put a stent and he has assured for the free flow of bile and he came out.

Patient is known hypertensive and IHG patient on antiplatelet drugs and it was stopped by us around five days prior to the surgery. And she had given a history of angioplasty in two thousand fifteen as well and she was non diabetic and non asthmatic.

We again subjected this patient for MRCP prior to going for any operative intervention and this MRCP showed the CVD stent in situ and two gallbladder calcula measuring around seven by four millimeters and multiple calcula seen in a common hepatic duct and common bile duct largest measuring around twelve by thirteen mm. and common bile duct was measuring around seventeen point six millimeter in diameter and pancreatic duct was also dilated and it was measuring about three point five mm in diameter

Our team for this particular patient is myself, Dr. Rahul Mahadhar, Dr. Sachin Vani was there to help me out for doing this particular procedure and interrupt interventions. And Dr. Dhanushya Mahadhar was a camera surgeon and Dr. Sonia Naik was an anesthetist. And Dr. Nirmal Dutta Thakur was a physician and intensivist to look after this patient in an immediate post-operative period.

The patient is positioned in a leg split position on the operating table. The surgeon stands between the patient's legs, with the assistant on the left side and the camera surgeon on the right side. Two monitors are placed at shoulder level on both sides. The port positions include a 12mm umbilical trocar, another 12mm trocar, and two 5mm trocars.

Following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum and trocar placement, the surgeon grasps the fundus of the gallbladder, encountering dense adhesions between the omentum and the gallbladder at the fundic region, as well as at the liver edges. These adhesions are carefully dissected using a harmonic scalpel, revealing a distended gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect and remove adhesions near the neck of the gallbladder.
The surgeon addresses adhesions between the duodenum and the distended, dilated common bile duct (CBD), using an Argonics device to carefully cut and separate the adhesions at the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the fat over the common bile duct (CBD), fully exposing the CBD.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the gallbladder from the common bile duct (CBD), carefully dissecting the adhesions between these structures.

The common bile duct (CBD) is well exposed. The surgeon uses a hook with monopolar current to mark and make an approximately two-centimeter incision over the CBD. Bile gushes out from the incision, which is suctioned out. The incision is then extended craniocaudally using a harmonic scalpel due to difficulty with monopolar current.
The surgeon extends the incision towards the hilum and porta hepatis, approximately two centimeters in length, following the initial incision.
A large stone is delivered from the incision in the common bile duct (CBD), followed by additional stones that are extracted with the aid of suction and manual pressure on the CBD.

The surgeon identifies a stone in the common hepatic duct and successfully delivers it out. Three stones are removed in total. A plastic bag is placed in the abdomen to collect and secure all the stones, preventing them from being misplaced.
The surgeon visually inspects the common hepatic duct, identifying a stent and a small stone. The stone is carefully removed using forceps.
The surgeon uses suction to irrigate the common hepatic duct, ensuring any remaining small stones are flushed out after clearing four stones from the duct.
The surgeon observes no stones in the common hepatic duct but identifies a stent extending into the left hepatic duct. The lower common bile duct is cleared, and two large stones are extracted.

The surgeon uses an intestinal grasp to push from the duodenal aspect, searching for stones in the lower CBD. A suction tip is employed to grab and push stones into the incision site, revealing one stone impacted in the lower CBD.

A flexible pediatric gastroscope is inserted into the common bile duct (CBD), revealing a large impacted stone at the lower end. The stone is tightly lodged alongside a stent, leaving no space for a catheter or balloon to pass through.

An adult gastroscope, approximately 9.3 mm in diameter, is inserted to visualize the stone in the common bile duct. The surgeon attempts to dislodge the stone using a foreign body grasping forceps, but the stone remains in place.

Under gastroscope guidance, a 4.5 mm French balloon extraction catheter is inserted into the lower common bile duct (CBD) below the impacted stone. The balloon is inflated to 15 mm, successfully dislodging the stone from its position. The stone is then pushed from the outside, allowing for its removal.

The surgeon observes the clearance of the lower common bile duct (CBD) using a pediatric gastroscope. An impacted stone is seen coming out through the previously made incision, and saline wash is applied to confirm the complete clearance alongside the visible stent.
The surgeon achieves complete clearance of the lower common bile duct (CBD) using a pediatric gastroscope. The right and left hepatic ducts are visualized, and a saline wash confirms their clearance.

Using a pediatric gastroscope, the surgeon confirms the position of the stent in the CBD and proceeds to close the CBD incision with a continuous suture using 2-0 Maxon. The stent remains in situ, and the closure is visually confirmed to be watertight.
After achieving hemostasis and closure of the common bile duct (CBD), the surgeon begins dissection of the hepatocystic triangle to proceed with gallbladder removal.
The surgeon begins dissection to locate the cystic duct and cystic artery, carefully separating them from the surrounding peritoneum on both the lateral and medial sides of the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes a large cystic duct with a significant diameter, noting its opening into the lower common bile duct (CBD) on the lateral aspect.
The surgeon continues dissection of the hepatocystic triangle using a hook and Maryland forceps, achieving simultaneous hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a Maryland forceps to dissect the cystic artery, which is visible below a lymph node. The cystic duct is also separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, ensuring clear visualization of the cystic duct and artery. Remaining adhesions are carefully dissected using a hook instrument.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hepatic cystic triangle, revealing the cystic duct, cystic artery, gallbladder, and gallbladder fossa. Adhesive tissue surrounding the cystic duct is meticulously cleared from all sides.
The surgeon coagulates the cystic artery using bipolar forceps, applies a clip, and then cuts the artery. The cystic duct is visibly cleared of surrounding tissue.
Hemoclips are applied to the cystic duct, which is then cut. The gallbladder is separated from the gallbladder fossa, with simultaneous hemostasis achieved during the procedure.
The surgeon places the separated gallbladder and eight stones into a retrieval bag and removes them through the umbilical port. The gallbladder and stones are visible in the surgical field.
